# Headless Commerce Migration: Solving Session Persistence in Strangler Fig Architectures

**Keyword:** monolith to headless commerce migration
**Date:** 2026-03-30
**Words:** 886
**QA:** 95/100

---

 Headless Commerce Migration: Orchestrating the Strangler Fig Pattern with  forensic post-mortem resolving  & Shared State  Most technical guides for a  monolith to headless commerce migration  focus on the UI benefits while ignoring the catastrophic failure point: shared state persistence. During a recent replatforming for a global retailer (1.2M MAU, 450 RPS peak), we encountered a critical session synchronization failure that led to an 18% spike in cart abandonment within hours of deploying the first phased routes. This case study breaks down the architecture required to prevent state drift when orchestrating a Next.js frontend alongside a legacy SAP Commerce Cloud backend.  1. INCIDENT REPORT: THE SESSION VOID  The migration utilized the  Strangler Fig Application  pattern, routing 30% of traffic (Product Detail Pages) to a Next.js instance while keeping the checkout flow on the legacy JSP monolith. Within 120 minutes of deployment, telemetry indicated a 22% failure rate in 'Add to Cart' actions for users transitioning between the new PDPs and the legacy checkout. The p95 latency for session hydration spiked from 110ms to 840ms, causing the Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) to timeout and return null session objects.  2. WRONG ASSUMPTIONS  Initial triage focused on cookie-domain configuration and SameSite attribute mismatches. We assumed the issue was a client-side browser rejection of the  JSESSIONID  when moving between the  shop.enterprise.com  (Next.js) and  checkout.enterprise.com  (Legacy) subdomains. However, logs showed the cookies were being sent correctly; the data within the session store simply didn't exist when requested by the secondary system.  3. DEBUGGING PROCESS  We implemented distributed tracing across the  proxy-layer orchestration  using OpenTelemetry. By injecting a  x-fouriq-trace-id , we monitored the request lifecycle across three layers:    Cloudflare Workers:  Acting as the routing proxy.   Next.js BFF:  Handling the  API-first architecture  calls.   Redis:  Serving as the shared session store between Java (Legacy) and Node.js (Next.js).   We discovered that while Next.js was writing to Redis, the legacy Java monolith utilized a deferred write-back strategy. The legacy system only flushed session updates to the database at the end of the request lifecycle, creating a race condition where the Next.js app was reading stale or empty data before the legacy write had finalized.  4. ROOT CAUSE  The failure was a synchronization lag inherent in the  data synchronization legacy  adapters. The legacy monolith expected to be the exclusive owner of the session. When the Next.js BFF modified the session (e.g., adding an item), it updated Redis immediately. However, when the user clicked 'Checkout' (Legacy), the monolith would overwrite the Redis key with its own locally cached (and now stale) session data, effectively wiping the user's cart. This is the 'Lost Update' problem in a distributed environment.  5. IMPLEMENTATION FIX: THE R
